coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive detection of Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) ions in water environment with a fluorescent sensor based on metal-organic frameworks combined with sulfur quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yanqiu
Liu, Jiaxiang
Wu, Xiaohan
Tao, Wenquan
Li, Zhuo
coredata.dc:description: Accurate, simple and quick detection methods for Cr(VI) detection are urgently needed for water quality monitoring. Herein, a novel and facile method of detecting Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) ions is developed via the fluorescent detection technology based on metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) doped with sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) (SQDs@UiO-66-NH2). The blue-light-emitting SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites exhibit excellent fluorescent properties in water environment with high quantum yield (68%) and ideal fluorescent stability, thus demonstrating excellent potential for serving as a chemical sensor. After characterizing the performance and stability of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2, qualitative and quantitative detection of Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− ions was successfully conducted. The fluorescence of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites in aqueous solution was quenched effectively with more than 90% quenching efficiency by Cr(VI) via the inner filter effect. The detection system provides considerable advantages such as rapid response (10 s), high sensitivity with a low detection limit of 0.16 μM in a broad linear range of 0–200 μM (R2 = 0.99) for Cr2O7 2− and 0.17 μM for CrO4 2− in a broad linear range of 0–220 μM (R2 = 0.99), high selectivity and reproducibility for at least five cycles with simple washing with alcohol. In practical applications, the sensor showed rapid response, high sensitivity and excellent recoveries (96.7%–105.4%) for detecting Cr2O7 2− in real water samples. Furthermore, a SQDs@UiO-66-NH2-based fluorescent test paper was successfully developed, providing a simple, reliable and portable method for Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) detection in water environment.

(a) Comparison of XRD patterns of UiO-66-NH2, SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 and SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 after detection Cr2O7 2−; (b) FTIR spectra of UiO-66-NH2, SQDs, SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 and SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 after detection Cr2O7 2−; (c) Full range XPS spectra of UiO-66-NH2 and SQDs@UiO-66-NH2BUC-14; XPS spectra of (d) Zr 3s; (e) S 2p; (f) O 1s.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate, simple and quick detection methods for Cr(VI) detection are urgently needed for water quality monitoring. Herein, a novel and facile method of detecting Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) ions is developed via the fluorescent detection technology based on metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) doped with sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) (SQDs@UiO-66-NH2). The blue-light-emitting SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites exhibit excellent fluorescent properties in water environment with high quantum yield (68%) and ideal fluorescent stability, thus demonstrating excellent potential for serving as a chemical sensor. After characterizing the performance and stability of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2, qualitative and quantitative detection of Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− ions was successfully conducted. The fluorescence of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites in aqueous solution was quenched effectively with more than 90% quenching efficiency by Cr(VI) via the inner filter effect. The detection system provides considerable advantages such as rapid response (10 s), high sensitivity with a low detection limit of 0.16 μM in a broad linear range of 0–200 μM (R2 = 0.99) for Cr2O7 2− and 0.17 μM for CrO4 2− in a broad linear range of 0–220 μM (R2 = 0.99), high selectivity and reproducibility for at least five cycles with simple washing with alcohol. In practical applications, the sensor showed rapid response, high sensitivity and excellent recoveries (96.7%–105.4%) for detecting Cr2O7 2− in real water samples. Furthermore, a SQDs@UiO-66-NH2-based fluorescent test paper was successfully developed, providing a simple, reliable and portable method for Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) detection in water environment.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most widely used metal ions in modern industry and agriculture, chromium, especially hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), is always stable in ecosystems [1,2]. However, Cr(VI) is highly toxic, easy to bioaccumulate and nonbiodegradable in water environment, and poses a significant risk to humans and most animals because it may cause hereditary genetic defects, allergic reactions and kinds of cancers even at trace or ultra-trace concentration [3,4]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (U.S.EPA) classified Cr(VI) as extremely toxic pollution, and the World Health Organization (WHO) regulated the threshold limit of Cr(VI) concentration as 0.96 μM in groundwater [5]. Thus, accurate detection and determination of Cr(VI) in water environment is essential to environmental protection and human security. Great efforts have been made to develop efficient methods for determining Cr(VI) in water environment by using techniques such as atomic absorption spectrum (AAS) [6,7], ion chromatography(IC) [8], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrum (ICP-MS) [9], and spectrophotometry [10]. Although these techniques are highly sensitive, most of them rely on large equipment and generally require tedious sample pretreatments as well as rich expertise and long time, thus limiting their feasibility for real-time detection. Facile, fast, sensitive and accurate detection method of Cr(VI) in water environment has hence become increasingly necessary. To better monitor the Cr(VI) in water environment, a number of novel chemosensors have been designed based on the boom of nanomaterials science in the last decade [3,11–13]. Among them, fluorescent sensors have captured numerous attention due to their high sensitivity and technical simplicity [12,14]. However, the response time, selectivity and portability of fluorescent sensors still need to be further improved.

MOFs, as a fascinating class of porous crystalline materials constructed by assembling metal ions with organic ligands, have aroused considerable interests due to their tunable pore size, high specific areas and self-assembling [15–17]. These unique properties make MOFs a promising candidate for gas storage [18], catalysis [19,20], sensing [21–23], drug delivery [24], adsorption and separation [25,26]. As for the application in Cr(VI) detection, Zhao et al. [27] synthesized a MOF containing 24-nuclear zinc nanocages for Cr(VI) detection, and the detection limit of CrO4 2− and Cr2O7 2− is 3 μM. Hu et al. [28] reported a luminescent Zn-MOF for Cr(VI) detection with detection limit of 0.3 and 0.7 ppm towards CrO4 2− and Cr2O7 2−, respectively. Yet, the detection limits of above reported sensors are relatively high, which hinders the practical application in sensing Cr(VI) with trace concentrations. Moreover, MOFs with intrinsic luminescence are restricted in real applications due to their low quantum yields, which is resulted from either the weak intrinsic luminescence of the metal and organic ligand, or the weak charge transfer between the metal and ligand [29]. Therefore, researchers have been making efforts to construct ideal luminescent MOF-based sensors with post-synthetic modification (PSM) approaches by loading functional materials on MOFs to enhance or generate unique properties on the basis of retaining the original MOF properties. UiO-66(Zr), composed of hexameric Zr6O32 units and linked by terephthalic acid, is a robust Zr-containing MOF material. UiO-66(Zr) is built up from octahedralcages with a diameter of 1.1 nm and tetrahedral cages with a diameter of 0.75 nm, which affords large surface area, large pore channel, and high chemical and thermal stability [29]. UiO-66-NH2, one of the functionalized UiO-66 frameworks, is isostructural to UiO-66 with the ligand changed from terephthalic acid to 2-NH2-terephthalic acid. UiO-66-NH2 retains the original properties of UiO-66 and serves as a unique host for various functional species modified because of the effective functionalization of the amino group [30].

Recently, luminescence quantum dots (QDs), with high quantum yields, long lifetimes, excellent photostability and size dependent emission wavelengths, have been seen as promising materials to fabricate various fluorescent sensors [31–33]. For instance, Willner et al. [34] prepared functionalized CdSe/ZnS QDs as fluorescent probes for the analysis of explosives. Wu et al. [35] developed a fluorescent biosensor for detecting lead(II) with fluorescence “turn on” based on energy transfer from CdSe/ZnS quantum dots to graphene oxide. Phenol formaldehyde resin nanoparticles loaded with CdTe QDs enable optical visual detection for copper(II) ions [36]. However, currently used heavy metal based QDs, such as CdTe and CdSe, suffer from toxicity and environmental hazards, limiting their potential use in real environment. Consequently, recent efforts have been made to develop non-toxic or heavy metal free QDs, especially pure elemental QDs, such as carbon QDs [37], graphene QDs [38], silicon QDs [39], black phosphorus QDs [40], and sulfur QDs [41]. Sulfur QDs (SQDs) with unique chemical properties and remarkable inherent biological activities, are promising next generation green nanomaterials. And sulfur, the third most abundant element in fossil fuels, is readily available. In addition, Shen et al. [41] synthesized SQDs with a quantum yield of 3.8% by a top-down method. Wang et al. [42] proposed an H2O2-assisted top-down method to synthesize SQDs with bright light emission and a quantum yield of up to 23%. The outstanding luminescent properties make SQDs attractive in sensing applications.

Herein, we report a facile room-temperature strategy to fabricate a luminescent sensor SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 for rapid (10 s), sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) in aqueous environments. SQDs@UiO-66-NH2, obtained by PSM approach, shows bright blue emission from the SQDs, and the fluorescence can be quickly quenched after the addition of Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) ions due to the inner filter effect (IFE) of Cr(VI). More importantly, the sensor enables accurate and quantitative determination of Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) over a broad linearity region at a detection limit of 0.16 μM for Cr2O7 2− and 0.17 μM for CrO4 2−. Based on an obvious, visible-to-naked-eye fluorescence change in color under a UV lamp, a simple fluorescent test paper was prepared and successfully applied to Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) detection. Furthermore, the developed sensor was successfully employed for the determination of Cr2O7 2− in real environment samples with satisfactory recoveries.

All chemicals involved were commercially available and of analytical grade without any additional purification. Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, 99%), and 2-NH2-terephthalic acid (H2ATA) (C8H7NO4, 99%), methyl alcohol (CH3OH, 99.9%), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (C3H7NO, 99.5%) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.99%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), sublimed sulfur (S, 99.9%), sodium chromate (Na2CrO4, 98%), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, 99%), chromium chloride hexahydrate (CrCl3·6H2O, 98%), iron (III) chloride (FeCl3, 99%), aluminum trichloride (AlCl3, 99%), cupric chloride anhydrous (CuCl2, 99%), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 98%), manganese (II) chloride (MnCl2, 99%), cobalt chloride (CoCl2, 99%), cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O, 99%), silver(I) chloride (AgCl, 98%), lead dichloride (PbCl2, 99%), polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG-400), sodium phosphate (Na3PO4, 99%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99%), sodium fluoride (NaF, 99%), sodium bromide (NaBr, 99%), sodium nitrate (NaNO3, 99%), sodium nitrite (NaNO2, 99%), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99%), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3, 99%), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, 99%), sodium acetate (CH3COONa, 99%), sodium oxalate (C2Na2O4, 99%), barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2·2H2O, 99%), sodium silicate (Na2SiO3, 99%) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Sodium formate (NaCHO2, 99%) was purchased from Adamas Reagent, Ltd. (China). Filter paper made of 100% cotton fiber was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China).

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded with a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer under CuKα radiation (λ = 0.15418 Å) at 40 mA current and 40 kV voltage with a 2θ scanning ranging from 5° to 50°. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were collected on a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer in the spectral range of 4000–400 cm−1 using the KBr disk method. The microstructure and elemental mapping were observed with a field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss, GeminiSEM 500) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI. The photoluminescence spectra were recorded at room temperature on a Thermo Scientific Lumina fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a 150 W Xenon lamp. Time-resolved photo-luminescence decay curves and the absolute luminescent quantum yields were recorded on an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (HACH DR-6000).

SQDs were prepared by an H2O2-assisted top-down approach [42]. Briefly, sublimed sulfur powder (1.4 g), ultrapure water (50 mL), PEG-400 (3 mL) and NaOH (4 g) were added into a 100 mL three necked round-bottom flask. This flask was placed on a magnetic stirrer with a temperature controller and fitted with a glass condenser. The mixture referred as “S dots” was obtained by stirring at 70 °C for 5 h. SQDs were prepared by mixing 1.5 mL S dots and 2.0 mL H2O2 solution (7.5 wt%) in a 15 mL centrifuge tube under vigorous stirring. SQDs emitted blue light under the radiation of UV-light.

UiO-66-NH2 was prepared via solvothermal route [43]. In this work, 0.2332 g ZrCl4 (1 mmol) and 0.1812 g H2ATA (1 mmol) were dissolved in 50 mL DMF and sealed in a 100 mL Teflon-line stainless steel autoclave which is heated to 120 °C and then remained constant for 48 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected by centrifugation and washed with DMF and methanol for three times each, and finally dried in vacuum at 100 °C for 12 h.

The prepared UiO-66-NH2 (50 mg) and SQDs (50 mL) were mixed together and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. After that, the solid was separated by centrifugation and washed with methanol three times to remove excess SQDs. The solid obtained afterwards was dried at 60 °C overnight.

The luminescence performances of UiO-66-NH2, SQDs and SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 were investigated. The luminescence stability was investigated by immersing 2 mg SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 in 2 mL deionized aqueous solution for 10 days and the luminescence spectra were recorded every day. The luminescence stability in aqueous solutions with different pH was also studied by immersing 2 mg powder SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 in 2 mL aqueous solutions with pH values ranging from 2.0 to 10.0.

The response kinetics was determined by the fluorescent intensity of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 at different time with immersing 2 mg SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 powder in 2 mL Cr2O7 2− (300 μM) aqueous solution at room temperature. The detection ability towards Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− was further determined, in a typical assay, 2 mg SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 was mixed with 2 mL different concentrations of Cr2O7 2− or CrO4 2−, and the fluorescence response was recorded under 340 nm excitation after incubating for 10 s at room temperature.

The interference effect of co-existing ions on Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− detection was further explored. 2 mg SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 powder was added in 2 mL different kinds of metal ions solutions (300 μM, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Ag+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+) or different anions solutions (300 μM, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CO3 2−, NO3 2−, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, CHO2 −, CH3COO−, SiO3 2−). The fluorescent intensities of above mixture solution with or without 300 μM Cr2O7 2− were separately measured after 10 s at room temperature. All data presented in the results are the averaged values of three parallel experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr2O7 2− ions in real samples detection
ce:section.ce:para: Real environmental samples were collected from river water (Tongji University), tap water (Tongji University) and source water (Qingcaosha raw water) in Shanghai. Those samples were filtered with a 0.45 μm pore size membrane (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China) to remove suspended particles and then spiked with various concentration of Cr2O7 2−. The fluorescence spectra were recorded with 2 mg SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 dispersed in 2 mL of the above solutions after 10 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites-based test paper
ce:section.ce:para: The filter paper was cut into strips of 1 cm × 2 cm, and the piece of filter paper was immersed in the SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 ethanol solution and ultrasonic for 10 min. Then the filter paper was removed from solution and dried at room temperature, and further used for Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− detection with the concentration of 300 μM.

Initially, the morphologies of UiO-66-NH2 and SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites were confirmed by SEM. As illustrated in Fig. 1 (a), large-scale UiO-66-NH2 particles with uniform sizes (500–600 nm) and regular octahedral shapes were obtained by the proposed synthetic route. After interacting with SQDs, SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites exhibit a rough surface morphology and the particle size tends to be less than 400 nm (Fig. 1(b and c)). Moreover, the elemental mapping (Fig. 1(d–h)) show that Zr, C, O, N, S are densely distributed on the entire material suggesting that SQDs were successfully loaded and well distributed on UiO-66-NH2.

The crystal structures of UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles and SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites were analyzed by XRD. Excellent crystallinity of UiO-66-NH2 was manifested in Fig. 2 (a), in which the major sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.36°, 8.48°, 12.04°, 14.15°, 17.08°, 22.25°, 25.68° and 33.12° were attributed to the (111), (002), (022), (113), (004), (115), (224), and (137) reflections of UiO-66, matching well with the simulated pattern, which indicates their crystalline phase purity [44]. For SQDs@UiO-66-NH2, the diffraction peaks at 7.36° and 8.48° disappeared and peaks at other temperatures were in disorder, suggesting the change of crystal structure of UiO-66-NH2. On the other hand, some new diffraction peaks emerged on the XRD pattern, reflecting the formation of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites.

The chemical composition of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites was further confirmed by FTIR data (Fig. 2(b)). The characteristic bands of UiO-66-NH2 at 3360 cm−1 and 1260 cm−1 occurred due to amino (v (N–H)) and amino (v (C–N)) groups. The adsorption peaks at 1576 and 1386 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of v
as (-COO-) and v
s (-COO-) of carboxylate group in BDC2−. And the adsorption peaks in the range of 600–800 cm−1 occurred due to Zr–O2 as longitudinal and transverse modes [44]. For the prepared SQDs@UiO-66-NH2, the additional absorption peaks were observed at 1118 and 1000 cm−1, which is attributed to stretching vibration of C–O–H or C–O–C in both PEG and SQDs samples [42], indicating the immobilized of SQDs on the framework. Because of the combination with H2O molecular by H-bonds, the band v (N–H) becomes a broad peak and lies in the region of 3448 cm−1. The band around 1576 cm−1 of UiO-66-NH2 is overlapped by the wide bank at 1634 cm−1 of SQDs; the peak around 1386 cm−1 is much weaker than that in UiO-66-NH2; and the band at 768 cm−1 disappears. These substantial changes further indicate that a relevant distortion of the Zr–O–Zr units constituting the MOF framework has appeared and new SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites were formed.

Subsequently, the surface chemical state and elemental composition of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 were investigated by XPS measurements. All the binding energies obtained from XPS analysis were corrected by referencing C1s to 284.8 eV. The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 2(c) confirms the existence of Zr, O, N, C and S in the SQDs@UiO-66-NH2, corresponding to the above element mapping results in Fig. 1(d)–(h). The curves in the Zr 3d region (Fig. 2(d)) could be deconvoluted into two peaks for Zr (3d5/2 and 3d3/2) located at 182.63 and 185.06 eV, respectively, indicating the existence of Zr4+ [45,46]. It shifts slightly compared with pristine UiO-66-NH2 peaks, which are located at 182.81 and 185.24 eV, respectively. The peaks at 161.46 and 163.06 eV on SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 were assigned to the atomic sulfur and the binding energy at 167.52 and 169.07 eV was attributed to the sulfur of SO3 2−(2p2/3) and SO3 2−(2p1/2), respectively (Fig. 2(e)), which is similar to previous report [42]. The XPS spectrum of O 1s orbital can be fitted by two peaks at binding energies of 531.73 and 529.96 eV (Fig. 2(f)), which are attributed to the oxygen components on the carboxylate group of H2ATA linkers and Zr–O bonds of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2, respectively, showing slight shift to lower values than that of UiO-66-NH2. These results not only demonstrate the existence of strong interaction between SQDs and UiO-66-NH2 in SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites, but also indicate the increase of electron cloud density of UiO-66-NH2 in SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites.

The luminescence performance of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 was exhaustively studied. The pristine UiO-66-NH2 is yellow powder under nature light, and has a weak emission peak at approximately 451 nm (Fig. 3 (a)). SQDs shows a maximum fluorescence emission at 441 nm (Fig. 3(a)) with blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, and the fluorescence point in the CIE chromaticity diagram is situated in the blue region (Fig.S1, b: X = 0.17, Y = 0.06). As shown in Fig. 3(a), SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite is white powder under nature light (inset, left) and displayed the maximum absorption peak at 430 nm (Ex = 340 nm) with strong blue fluorescence(inset, right), which is visible to naked eye as demonstrated in the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig.S1, a: X = 0.15, Y = 0.08), indicating that SQDs are doped into the composites and the blue fluorescence originates from the SQDs. There is a blue shift of 11 nm of the maximum emission of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite (430 nm) compared with that of SQDs, indicating that there is hydrogen bonds or coordination interactions between the SQDs and UiO-66-NH2 rather than physical interactions [47]. The luminescence quantum yield of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 (68%) was increased nearly 3 times those of SQDs (23%) in previous report [42]. Luminescence decay curves of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 are shown in Fig. 3(b), in which the related lifetime was obtained by a single-exponential fitting, and the luminescence lifetime prolonged from 1.91 ns to 2.73 ns for the bare SQDs [42] to 15.97 ns for SQDs incorporated on UiO-66-NH2 in our work. In addition, the fluorescence emission of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 is excitation-dependent, and the emission intensity gradually increased with the excitation wavelength increasing from 310 to 340 nm and decreased at longer excitation wavelengths, as presented in Fig.S2. This typical excitation-dependent fluorescence emission is similar to those of previously reported QDs [48,49].

The fluorescence stability of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 was also investigated by monitoring its emission intensity at different time. In 10 days, no obvious fluorescence quenching was observed, indicating the good stability of the sensor (Fig.S3). Furthermore, the characteristic emission of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 in aqueous solution shows favorable luminescence stability in a wide pH range from 4 to 10, and has a red shift in the solution whose pH = 2 (Fig.S4). Thus, SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 with strong luminescence signal and excellent luminescence stability is a promising fluorescence sensor candidate in water pollution monitoring.

In order to study the response kinetics, 2 mg SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 powder was immersed in 2 mL Cr2O7 2− (300 μM) ion aqueous solution at room temperature and the fluorescent intensity was measured at different time. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , the emission intensity of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 significantly declined with the presence of Cr2O7 2− (300 μM) in 10 s, indicating the fluorescence response of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 for Cr(VI) is exceedingly fast and can reach balance within 10 s. Thus, all the sensing properties of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 towards Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) in this work were measured in real time.

In addition, the concentration dependence quenching effect of Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) on SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 forms the basis of quantitative detection. Thus, the feasibility of Cr(VI) detection was further explored by dispersing SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 (2 mg) into 2 mL aqueous solution of Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) with different concentrations, and the fluorescence responses were recorded. In Fig. 5 (a) and (c), the emission peaks at 430 nm were homogenously decreased along with the increase of Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− concentration from 0 to 300 μM under the excitation of 340 nm. The fluorescence quenching both reached more than 90%, and the color of fluorescence changing under ultraviolet light can be visually represented in Fig. 5(a) (inset). The Stern-Volmer plots [50] (Eq. (1)) for Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− are presented in Fig. 5(b) and (d), respectively.
(1) (I 0 / I) = 1+K sv[M]
where I 0 and I are the luminescence emission intensities of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 and SQDs@UiO-66-NH2-analytes suspensions, respectively; [M] represents the molar concentration of analytes (μM); and K sv is the Stern-Volmer constant (M−1).

A good linear correlation exists over the range of 0–200 μM for Cr2O7 2− (Fig. 5(b)) and 0–220 μM for CrO4 2− (Fig. 5(d)). The K sv value of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 for Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− was calculated as 3.12 × 104 M−1 and 2.92 × 104 M−1, respectively. The detection limit was calculated as 0.16 μM for Cr2O7 2− and 0.17 μM for CrO4 2−, according to the IUPAC 3σ method (LOD = 3σ/K) [51], where σ is the standard deviation of 11 blank measurements, and K is the slope of the calibration curve (K sv). This value is much lower than the maximum allowable concentration (0.96 μM) of Cr(VI) in drinking water permitted by the World Health Organization (WHO) [5], and also lower than the permitted maximum level of integrated wastewater discharge (0.5 mg L−1) based on the Chinese National Standard [12]. To protect national nature reserves, China surface water quality is classified into five levels, and the detection limit is lower than the concentration regulated in the highest standard (0.19 μM, GB3838-2002, Grade I) (Table S1). Thus, this method is sensitive enough to detect Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) ions in water environment. Moreover, the sensitivity of this fluorescent sensing platform is comparable to other quantum dots or MOF materials based fluorescent sensing systems for Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) as summarized in Table 1 , and the corresponding linear range of Cr(VI) concentration is broader than most of other methods, indicating the high potential of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 in monitoring Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) ions.

The form of Cr(VI) in water is related to pH. As pH in the range of 2–6, there is equilibrium between Cr2O7 2− and HCrO4− ions, and under alkaline conditions (pH > 8) there are predominantly CrO4 2− ions. The influence of pH on Cr(VI) detection was explored, by dispersing SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 (2 mg) into 2 mL aqueous solution of Cr2O7 2− (300 μM) with different pH (2–12) at room temperature. The result was presented in Fig.S5, in which the quenching efficiency remained high at 91%–92% as the pH in the range of 4–12. The quenching efficiency decreased very slightly with 89% as pH at 2. Thus, SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 can be used for Cr(VI) detection in water environment with a wide pH range.

To investigate the selectivity of the SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 sensor, fifteen other metal ions including Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Ag+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Ba2+ were selected as interfering agents. In this experiment, 2 mg SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 solid sample was dispersed into a 2 mL aqueous solution containing 300 μM of above-mentioned ions, and the emission peak at 430 nm was recorded. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), upon the addition of Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− and fourteen other ions (Fe3+ not included), only Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− can lead to remarkable quenching on the luminescence of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2, whereas the quenching effect induced by other ions was almost negligible. Since pollutant ions often coexist in environmental waters, the luminescence test of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 was further performed with mixed ions, containing Cr2O7 2− (300 μM) and one of the other thirteen metal ions (300 μM) (Fe3+ not included). Results in Fig. 6(b) show that the luminescent intensity of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 in the solution with mixed ions is still comparable to that in pure Cr2O7 2− solution, indicating the recognition of Cr2O7 2− with SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 is free from the interference of other metal ions.

To further check the potential of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 as a fluorescent sensor for Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) detection in water environment, the fluorescence response towards potential interfering anions including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CO3 2−, NO3 2−, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, CHO2 −, CH3COO− and SiO3 2− were also investigated. Fig. 6(c) reveals that only the presence of Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− can achieve significant quenching effect. And the fluorescence quenching of Cr2O7 2− was not affected even by other co-existed anions (Fig. 6(d)).

In addition, the concentrations of some common ions in water bodies are actually higher than 300 μM (Table S2), thus Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, HCO3 2−, NO3 2−, SO4 2−, Cl− with high concentration (10 mM) were chosen to test the potential interference for the detection of Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− with SQDs@UiO-66-NH2. The results in Fig.S6 show that the quenching effect induced by the above-mentioned ions with high concentration was almost negligible, and the quenching effect induced by Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− were not influenced by these co-existing ions in high concentrations.

Additionally, Fe3+ may also result in obvious fluorescence quenching of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 and influence the quantitative determination of Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) (Fig.S7). So we use NaF solution to remove Fe3+ since excessive Na+ and F− could not affect the fluorescence of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2. Results in Fig.S7 demonstrate that no obvious fluorescence quenching was observed of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 in 2 mL Fe3+ solution (300 μM) after the addition of excessive NaF. Moreover, for the solution containing 300 μM Fe3+ and 300 μM Cr2O7 2−, the fluorescence of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 was quickly quenched and the quenching efficiency was similar to that of the solution with 300 μM Cr2O7 2−. This result confirms that the quantitative detection of Cr2O7 2− was not interfered at all after the removal of Fe3+ by NaF. The above interference experiments clearly elucidate superior selectivity of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 towards Cr(VI) in water environment, suggesting high feasibility in real water environment.

Since SQDs have obvious fluorescence signal, the detection of metal ions with SQDs was further explored in contrast. Fourteen metal ions (300 μM) mentioned above were separately added to 2 mL SQDs solution, and the emission peak at 441 nm was recorded (Fig.S8). Apart from Cr2O7 2−and CrO4 2−, the Cr3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, and Ag+ all have relatively strong quenching effect on the fluorescence of SQDs. The results demonstrate that the combination of SQDs with UiO-66-NH2 can improve the selectivity in the detection of Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) in water environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 for Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the literature review, the fluorescence quenching effect caused by ions is mainly due to the following factors: (i) the collapse of the crystal structure [58]; (ii) the interaction between the target ions and the organic ligands in sensor materials [59]; (iii) the interaction between the target ions and metal ions in sensor materials [60]; (iv) the overlap between the absorption spectrum of the target analytes and the excitation spectrum of the sensor materials [52]; (v) the overlap between the absorption spectrum of the target analytes and the fluorescence emission spectrum of sensor materials [13]. To explore the mechanism for fluorescence quenching with Cr(VI), further measurements have been performed. The structure of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites after detection experiments was analyzed with XRD tests. After the detection of Cr2O7 2−, the XRD pattern of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 is well consistent with that of the composites (Fig. 2(a)), thus excluding the possibility of (i). Moreover, the lifetime of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 in aqueous solutions in the absence and presence of Cr2O7 2− was explored. As revealed in Fig. 3(b), in the absence and presence of Cr2O7 2−, the average lifetime were 15.97 ns and 14.41 ns, respectively, which is basically consistent and proves to be a static quenching process. In the quenching process, the resonance energy transfer mechanism usually makes the donor fluorescence lifetime decline significantly. So, the consistency of the lifetime proves that the resonance energy transfer doesn’t play a crucial role in this process [14], which further rules out the possibility of (ii) and (iii). Then, the UV–vis absorption spectra of aqueous solutions of the chosen metal ions and anions were recorded. As shown in Fig. 6(e) and (f), the excitation spectrum of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 was covered by the absorption spectrum of Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2−, while other metal ions (expect Fe3+) and anions have no obvious absorption at these wavelengths (340 nm). As a consequence, on the basis of the IFE of Cr(VI), Cr2O7 2− and CrO4 2− can shield the excitation light of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2, hence resulting in a decrease of fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, according to previous reports, the sensing approach based on the IFE usually exhibits higher sensitivity and faster response compared with the resonance energy transfer [14].

In addition, there is no new adsorption peaks on the FTIR spectra of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 after the detection of Cr2O7 2− in contrast to that of the prepared composites, indicating the O–H…O hydrogen bonding interactions between SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 and Cr2O7 2− ions is very weak. The above speculation can be verified by the pleasurable recyclability of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 7 ), which is easily refreshed with alcohol washing after the detection experiment and can be reused in the following runs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing Cr2O7 2− in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 in practical applications, three real water samples from source water, tap water and river water were collected to determine Cr2O7 2− according to standard addition way. 2 mg SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 was mixed with 2 mL water samples with various contents of Cr2O7 2−, and the fluorescence responses were measured after incubated for 10 s at room temperature. Table 2 reveals that the obtained recoveries are in the range of 96.7%–105.4%. The relative standard deviations (RSD) of the spiked samples were calculated to be from 0.93% to 3.13% which confirmed the accuracy of our detection methods. In addition, the fluorescence background at 540 nm in water samples under UV illumination is negligible, which further confirms that the detection method based on SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 is promisingly applicable to detect Cr(VI) ions in real water samples.

To make the detection simple and portable, SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 based fluorescent test paper was developed. The test paper shows bright blue light under 340 nm UV light irradiation. When soaked in Cr2O7 2− or CrO4 2− aqueous solutions with concentrations of 300 μM for seconds, the fluorescent colors of the test paper changed from bright blue to dark blue (Fig. 8 ). Other chosen metal ions or anions (300 μM) were also detected with the test paper, and no obvious fluorescent color change was found (Figs. S9 and S10).

Taking Cr2O7 2− ion as example, the test papers changed from bright blue to dark blue as soaked in 20, 60, 100, 150, 200, 300 μM Cr2O7 2− aqueous solutions, and also changed to dark blue in tap water with 300 μM Cr2O7 2− added (Fig. S11). To naked eyes, the color changed with or without Cr2O7 2− can be distinguished, confirming the reliability of the test paper for qualitative detection of Cr(VI) in water environment.

In summary, we have developed a luminescent sensor (SQDs@UiO-66-NH2) based on UiO-66-NH2 via PSM method for the detection of trace amount of Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) ions in aqueous solutions. It is found that SQDs successfully dope on UiO-66-NH2 and give a strong and stable blue light emission to SQDs@UiO-66-NH2 composites in water environment. The fabricated sensor can be satisfactorily applied to the determination of Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) ions with a short response time (10 s), high sensitivity, superior selectivity and acceptable repeatability. The detection limit obtained with 0.16 μM for Cr2O7 2− and 0.17 μM for CrO4 2− in a wide linear range is far below the threshold proposed by WHO. In particular, the change in fluorescent signal stimulated by UV light is strong and visible to the naked eye, and a simple Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) ions test paper was prepared based on SQDs@UiO-66-NH2, making the sensor portable for practical Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) detection. The detection mechanism is proposed with the inner filter effect (IFE) of Cr(VI), which usually has high sensitivity and quick response. The potential in practical applications was further highlighted with good recoveries (96.7%–105.4%) obtained in real water samples with spiked of Cr2O7 2− ions. This work provides a reliable and simple method for Cr(VI) (Cr2O7 2−/CrO4 2−) detection, and we believe this work will extend the viewpoint in the design and construction of accurate methods for Cr(VI) monitoring in water environment.
